David allowed Absalom's display. Parents do not know what they are doing when they indulge a proud nature in their children; many young people are ruined by pride. And those who commonly desire authority the least understand its duties.

See how willing, loving parents are to believe the best about their children. But how easy and how wicked it is for children to take advantage of good parents, and to deceive them with a show of religion! The principal men of Jerusalem joined Absalom's feast upon his sacrifice. Pious people are glad to see others appear religious, and this gives opportunity for deceptions. The policy of wicked people, and the subtlety of Satan, are used to draw good people to support dishonorable plans.

David decided to leave Jerusalem. He made this decision as a penitent submitting to punishment. Before unrighteous Absalom he could justify himself, and stand firm; but before the righteous God he must condemn himself, and yield to his judgments. Thus he accepts the punishment of his sin. And good people, when they themselves suffer, are anxious that others should not be led to suffer with them. He compelled no one; whoever’s heart was with Absalom, let them go to Absalom, and so shall their fate be. Thus Christ enlists only willing followers. David cannot bear to think that Ittai, a stranger and an exile, a proselyte and a new convert, who should be encouraged and made easy, should meet with harsh treatment. But Ittai values David's wisdom and goodness so much that he will not leave him. He is a friend indeed, who loves at all times, and will remain loyal to us in adversity. Let us commit ourselves to the Son of David with full purpose of heart, and neither life nor death shall separate us from his love.

David is very careful about the safety of the ark. It is right to be more concerned for the church’s prosperity than our own; to prefer the success of the gospel above our own wealth, reputation, comfort, and safety. Observe with what satisfaction and submission David speaks of God's plan. It is our interest as well as our duty cheerfully to accept God’s will, whatever happens to us. Let us see God's hand in all events; and so that we may not be afraid of what shall be, let us see all events in God’s hand. David's sin was always before him; but never so plain, nor ever appearing so black as now. He never wept thus when Saul hunted him, but a wounded conscience makes troubles lie heavy.

David did not pray against Ahithophel's person, but against his counsel. He prayed this, with firm belief that God has all hearts in his hand, and tongues also. But we must follow our prayers with effort, and David did so, else we tempt God. But we do not find wisdom and simplicity so united in any mere man, that we can perceive nothing which needs forgiveness. Yet, when the Son of David was treated with all possible treachery and cruelty, his wisdom, gentleness, honesty, and patience were perfect. Him let us follow, commit ourselves to, and serve, in life and in death.
